checks.a_analysis: The output JSON faithfully follows the textual content of the source document. It captures the main title, date, legend headers, legend items, and source information without adding any invented text or fabricated sections. The metadata title 'Map Imagery Logo Imagery F810' is an expected AI-generated addition for accessibility purposes.
The source document contains no contact information (phone numbers, emails, addresses, or URLs). The output JSON also contains no contact information, thus no fabrications occurred.

All sections of the single-page source document are represented in the output. The textual components (title, date, legend, sources) are captured as headings, lists, or paragraphs. The graphical components (the map and the logo) are captured as image elements.
There are no tables in the source document. The legend, while presented in a columnar format in the source, is correctly represented as lists in the output JSON, preserving all 8 status categories.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: The output JSON captures all the primary text content. While the county labels, scale bar text, and north arrow indicator present on the map graphic are not extracted as separate text elements, they are part of the map image itself, which is included in the output. This is standard practice for semantic conversion of maps and diagrams. All standalone text sections (Title, Legend, Sources) are complete.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The text in the output JSON matches the source document exactly in terms of wording and data (e.g., dates like 'January 8, 2025' and legend items like 'Processing for 2025 Q1'). No meaning-altering changes were found.

checks.a_analysis: I have reviewed the output JSON and the source PDF for any duplicated content. All text elements in the output, including the title, date, legend items, and source information, appear exactly as they do once in the source document. There are no repeated blocks or sections.

The output correctly identifies the main title as an H1 and the date as an H2. This is a logical hierarchy following the layout of the source document where the title is the primary heading and the date acts as a subtitle. There are no skipped levels.
The source document does not contain any tables. The legend information is correctly interpreted as list content rather than a table.

The reading order in the output follows a logical top-down, left-to-right flow: Title, Date, Legend, Sources, and finally the Image. In the source, the legend and sources are positioned at the bottom left, while the main content is the map. Placing textual information before the image they describe (the map) is an acceptable adjustment for accessibility and maintains the logical flow of the information.

The legend section of the map, which lists the parcel update quarters, has been converted into unordered lists. The items in these lists correctly correspond to the text entries in the source legend (e.g., 'Processing for 2025 Q1', '2024 Q4', etc.). While the legend labels 'Parcel Updates:' and 'Most recent Quarter' are included as list items (elements 3 and 4), this captures the informational content of the legend area accurately.
The source document contains no visible URLs or hyperlinks. No links have been injected into the output JSON.

checks.a_analysis: The source document is a map-centric status report where the visual map is the primary content, positioned prominently between the title and the legend. In the HTML output, the visual organization is fundamentally altered. The legend items are converted into a bulleted list and placed above the sources, while the map itself (which is the core information) is relegated to the bottom as a broken image icon. This reversal of flow and the loss of the primary graphical element constitutes a major organization difference.
checks.b_evidence_source: The source document presents a large map of North Carolina counties as the central feature, with the legend and logos positioned around it to provide context.
checks.b_evidence_output: The HTML output presents the legend as a list of text ('Processing for 2025 Q1', '2024 Q4', etc.) followed by the sources, with the map represented only by a broken image placeholder at the very end of the content.

checks.a_analysis: There are no tables present in the source PDF; the document consists of a map, a legend (which is a list of color-coded categories), and text blocks.

The most critical visual elements of the document are either broken or missing in the HTML. The map of North Carolina, which contains the actual data of the report, is shown as a broken image icon. The NCGICC logo is also a broken image. Furthermore, the globe logo, the North arrow, and the scale bar (all meaningful spatial and branding information) are missing entirely from the rendering.
checks.b_evidence_source: The PDF contains a colored map of NC counties, a north arrow, a scale bar, and two logos in the bottom right corner (NCGICC and a globe graphic).
checks.b_evidence_output: The HTML displays broken image icons with alt text: 'A map of North Carolina counties colored according to the status of their parcel updates' and 'NCGICC logo'. The globe logo, north arrow, and scale bar have no corresponding elements.

checks.a_analysis: There is no evidence of body content duplication in the HTML output. Each segment of text and the image placeholders appear only once.
